_doc: Recurring failure patterns + methodology + Berkeley-mapping rule for the #194 stratified hand-audit. Consumed by scripts/audit_failure_taxonomy.py render. Update this sidecar when re-auditing; render emits the markdown sections from this file.

methodology.title: Sampling design — coverage, not prevalence
methodology.body: The 46 audited rows are a `(experiment_cell, auto_taxonomy_label)` stratified sample produced by `scripts/build_failure_taxonomy.py:stratified_sample` — one row per non-empty stratum across the 1,276 paper-eligible failures, deterministic seed 20260506. **Counts in this doc measure coverage of strata, not prevalence over the failure population.** A line like 'task_verification appears in 34 of 43 audited-with-label rows' means the auditor labeled 34 strata as task_verification — it does NOT mean ~79% of the 1,276 failures are task_verification. For a prevalence estimate, run a separate random-sample audit weighted by stratum size, or extrapolate weighted counts from the per-stratum confirmed-rate scaled by auto-label prevalence in the full 1,276 rows. The recurring-pattern row counts below are rows-in-this-sample; the parenthetical 'across N distinct scenarios' is the orthogonal coverage measure that distinguishes 'one buggy scenario replicated across many cells/models' from 'a broad-distributional failure mode'.

berkeley_mapping_rule.title: Berkeley-label mapping rule (this audit)
berkeley_mapping_rule.body: Per `docs/failure_taxonomy_evidence.md:139-145` tie-break rule, when more than one Berkeley label is plausible the auditor picks **the latest irreversible mistake visible in the artifact** and mentions upstream contributors in the audit_note. Concretely: (a) if the trajectory's tool-call sequence is materially wrong but the agent never reaches a final-answer-with-fabrication, label as `inter_agent_orchestration` at the failing tool stage; (b) if the agent emits a final answer / WO / decision that depends on missing, empty, or wrong evidence (i.e. the upstream gap was not corrected before the final answer landed), the latest irreversible mistake is the unearned final answer — label as `task_verification (final_answer)` even when there's an upstream tool_selection or tool_execution contributor. (c) `specification` is reserved for cases where the agent misunderstood the task itself (per the team-doc definition); the auditor did not see this in the 46-row sample. v3 of this audit relabeled six rows that the v2 first pass labeled by upstream stage — see `tie_break_relabels` below.

tie_break_relabels._doc: Six rows that v2 labeled by 'first irreversible mistake' (per auditor's initial reading) and v3 relabeled by 'latest irreversible mistake' per the team's tie-break rule.

rows.rationale: WO emission at end is the latest irreversible mistake; wrong sensor type 'dga_h2_ppm' was an upstream contributor that the agent partially recovered from.
Same shape as run02; wrong sensor name was upstream, the unearned WO is the latest mistake.
Final answer asserts a 10-healthy/10-degraded breakdown that the agent never retrieved (no get_sensor_readings calls); the unearned breakdown is the latest mistake.
Same shape as B70B run3; only T-005 was fully populated, but the agent emits per-unit fleet breakdown.
v2 had berkeley correct but stage at planning; latest irreversible mistake is the unearned breakdown, so stage moves to final_answer.
Same shape as Z70B run1; stage corrected from planning to final_answer.

lead: These patterns recur across multiple audited rows and are the strongest paper-citable signals. Each row's count is rows-in-this-sample and 'across M distinct scenarios' to flag patterns where a single scenario reproduces across multiple cells/models (cross-model robustness signal) versus broad-distributional (corpus-coverage signal):

patterns.title: Wrong sensor name (`winding_temp_c` vs canonical `winding_temp_top_c`)
patterns.description: The 8B and 70B agents both pick a non-existent sensor name when asked about thermal trends. Tool returns 'no readings', but the agent then proceeds to outage-WO creation anyway.
patterns.mitigation: validate `sensor_id` against `iot.list_sensors` output before `iot.get_sensor_readings` / `tsfm.trend_analysis`, and hard-fail on unknown sensors instead of returning empty.

patterns.title: Low-temp + Arc Discharge contradiction
patterns.description: The agent diagnoses 'low-temperature overheating' (a thermal fault) and matches it to FM-006 'Arc Discharge' (a high-energy electrical fault) — physically incompatible classes. Now spans Y70B / YS_TP / YS70B / Z70B / ZS70B (every PE-family 70B variant on SGT-014).
patterns.mitigation: enforce a fault-mode-class consistency check between `analyze_dga` output and `search_failure_modes` result; reject thermal-class diagnoses paired with electrical-class FM matches.

patterns.title: T-006 vs T-008 fault-record confusion
patterns.description: `get_fault_record(F006)` returns a T-008 record (since F006 belongs to T-008 in the fixture); the task asked about T-006; agent emits T-008 details as the answer.
patterns.mitigation: validate that the returned record's `transformer_id` matches the task's named asset before downstream tools consume it.

patterns.title: WO creation without preceding diagnostic
patterns.description: Agent jumps straight to `create_work_order` (or after a single shallow tool call), skipping the discover/analyze/correlate sequence. The WO carries either fabricated severity / priority / playbook fields or empty placeholders. Z70B SGT-007 run01 also emits the WO despite explicit 'invalid severity' + session errors.
patterns.mitigation: gate `create_work_order` behind a 'minimum-evidence' precondition checker (analyze_dga and/or trend_analysis outcome present in the trajectory); refuse to mark an errored WO as success.

patterns.title: INT8 model emits invented failure-mode IDs
patterns.description: The INT8 8B variant on Cell D uses literal strings (`list_failure_modes`, `D2`, `FM-012`, `T1`) as `failure_mode_id` arguments, none of which exist.
patterns.mitigation: tool-input validation that rejects unknown IDs before the call dispatches.

patterns.title: Fleet-status snapshot covers only one representative unit
patterns.description: Task asks for healthy + degraded (or healthy + degraded + critical) representative samples; agent fully populates only one unit (typically T-005 or T-011) and asserts the breakdown without per-unit retrieval.
patterns.mitigation: in PE/verified-PE planning, expand 'representative units' to a numbered list and require one `iot.get_sensor_readings` call per declared unit before final aggregation.

summary_note: All 6 patterns are 1-3-distinct-scenario clusters reproducing across cells / models / orchestration paths. Total: 26 pattern-rows across 6 distinct scenarios (SGT-007, SGT-009, SGT-010, SGT-014, SGT-018, SGT-022). The `specification` Berkeley category was not assigned to any audited row in this sample, applying the team's narrow definition (agent misunderstanding the task itself). Reasonable readers may argue patterns §1 (wrong sensor name) and §3 (T-006 vs T-008) sit on the boundary between `task_verification` and a hypothetical `tool_environment_specification` sub-label — the auditor's read is that the task is precisely specified and the gap is in the agent's tool-environment surface (canonical sensor names, asset-record consistency), not in the task statement, but a methodology revisit could re-draw the boundary.
